artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

I’m still at the office finishing the council packet stuff and needed to get this off my chest somewhere that isn’t a staff meeting.

This whole Unit #27 saga has been simmering for a couple of years, but I’m only really shoving it onto the front burner now because residents have started going around me. In the last month we’ve had three people from the Oak Street/Mississippi Parkway area emailing council members directly and copying the Dispatch, all referencing the December 9, 2024 transmission failure by Brainerd High School and the February 2025 breakdown on Mississippi Parkway. Kelly keeps forwarding them with “please respond ASAP” and it’s wearing me down.

It still bugs me that on Dec. 9 Jeff Olson and I sat on the shoulder by the high school for nearly three hours while the truck cooled off, then a week later, on December 16, AFSCME filed that grievance over the 14‑hour shifts we needed to cover the northeast route. Since then, Kelly and Mark Jensen have been on me about avoiding another mess like that.

Then there was the January 5, 2025 scare on Oak by 1421, when #27 lost hydraulic pressure and slid toward that family. The dad, James McAllister, called my personal cell around 9:30 p.m. and threatened to talk to his attorney at Brainerd Lakes Law Group if we kept running “that dangerous junk truck” on his block. That one still wakes me up at night.

I know you’re tired of hearing about trucks at the dinner table. Just trying to remind myself why I’m pushing so hard to get this on the March 16, 2026 agenda while we’re also juggling Noah’s college stuff and Claire’s choir. I’ll be home by 7 if nothing else blows up.

– M

metadata.subject: Why I’m pushing this truck so hard

artifacts.content:
Work journal – 2024-10-21

Quick debrief after meeting in Kelly Steele’s office at City Hall.

Kelly pulled me in this afternoon to talk about the two resident complaints from Oak Street and the Mississippi Parkway neighborhoods. She tied both directly to delayed plowing when Unit #27 was down last winter. She was pretty blunt that if we have another season of route delays tied to that truck, it could show up in my 2025–26 evaluation and jeopardize the 3% merit increase. Not a threat exactly, but I heard it.

This is on top of everything that’s already happened: March 2024 ice storm where we had to call Dad at his place off County Road 45 in the middle of the night so he could talk Tyler Benson through manually overriding the failing salter controls on #27. The union steward made a quiet comment afterward about me bringing in “family help” off the books. I can’t do that again, especially after Dad’s stroke at St. Joseph’s on April 3, 2023 and Dr. Ellison limiting him to short shifts.

Then there was February 2025 on Mississippi Parkway – #27 died again, I worked a 16‑hour day and missed Claire’s 14‑year‑old checkup with Dr. Amanda Schultz at Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s. Em said Dr. Schultz thinks Claire’s asthma is being aggravated by the stress of me being gone so much during storms and her worry about me being out in failing equipment.

Note to self: build a strong operational and financial case for the 2026 replacement in the CIP, but keep all of this personal stuff out of the formal documents.

metadata.title: Work journal – Unit #27 and Kelly meeting

artifacts.content:
To: sarah.peterson@email.com
Subject: Dad, storms, and that cursed truck

Hey Sis,

Catching up while I wait for a call back from the Fleet Supervisor.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how much we’ve leaned on Dad over the years. During the March ’24 ice storm, I actually called him at his place off County Road 45 in the middle of the night so he could coach our new driver, Tyler Benson, through manually overriding the busted salter controls on Unit #27. The union steward grumbled later about me pulling in “family help,” and honestly, with Dad’s mild stroke at St. Joe’s on April 3, 2023 and Dr. Ellison telling him no overnights and only short (under 4‑hour) shifts, I shouldn’t have asked.

I also briefly toyed with the idea of having him cover a west‑side route through his “Larson Driveway & Lot Service” like he did informally during the March 2023 heavy snow, but it’s a conflict of interest and just not fair to his health.

On the home front, Claire’s anxiety has been more noticeable since that February 9–11, 2024 blizzard when I was out for 18‑hour shifts three days in a row. Emily had to handle a late‑night panic episode alone because her counselor, Lisa McDermott at Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic, wasn’t available. That’s one of the reasons I’m pushing so hard to replace #27 now – fewer breakdowns should mean less crazy overtime and more predictable nights at home.

Honestly, we haven’t spent a ton of time dreaming up fancy alternatives beyond the new truck. Everyone’s stretched thin, and I just don’t have the bandwidth to run a bunch of scenarios when I’m already juggling Dad’s follow‑ups and Noah’s senior year.

Call when you get a minute.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Dad, storms, and that cursed truck

artifacts.content:
To: angela.ruiz@ci.brainerd.mn.us
Subject: Documentation – Halvorson contractor complaint (Dec 19, 2023)

Hi Angela,

Following up on our conversation after the visit with the Halvorsons.

On December 19, 2023, around 7:30 p.m., we received a complaint from resident Mark Halvorson on Oak Street. He reported that one of our regular snow‑removal contractors made a derogatory remark about his son’s disability while plowing their cul‑de‑sac. I met with Mark and his wife at their home on December 21 to hear the details and apologize in person. Per your guidance, I’ve reminded the contractor of our expectations and documented the incident in their file.

Incidents like this are a big part of why I’m so hesitant to lean more heavily on contractors as an “alternative” to replacing Unit #27. While we do use them for some cul‑de‑sacs and alleys, I’d rather keep primary routes in‑house where we have more direct control over operator conduct.

We’ve also kicked around, internally, the idea of just muddling along with the status quo and hoping the Oak and Pine folks quiet down over time, mostly to avoid more political fireworks. But that’s not really a solution, and it doesn’t sit well with me given what happened with Halvorson and the other complaints.

Let me know if you need anything else on this for HR records.

Thanks,

Michael M. Larson
Public Works Director

Personal meeting prep – Council motions and my own mess

Jotting down some possible motion language before the P&F Committee packet goes out.

– “Move to adopt the resolution authorizing the purchase of one (1) International HV507 SFA single‑axle plow truck with Henderson dump body, stainless steel sander, underbody scraper, and plow package from Boyer Trucks of Duluth through the State CPV program in an amount not to exceed $313,000, including sales tax and delivery, and authorize staff to declare Unit #27 surplus and dispose of it via public auction.”

– Another option I keep circling: “Move to approve staff’s recommendation, recognizing we might have to come back later and clean up the scope or budget if we missed something.” Not ideal, but realistic.

I have to remember not to drag my personal baggage into the public discussion: my 2019 DWI on Washington Street and the 12‑month ignition interlock – that’s part of why I’m hyper‑focused on safe winter roads, but it doesn’t belong at the podium. Same with Noah’s stuff: January 16, 2024 when Unit #27 went down on Mill Avenue at 4:15 a.m. and Emily and Noah were 25 minutes late to his 8:00 a.m. diversion check‑in with Daniel Ortiz at the Crow Wing County Justice Center for the October 2023 shoplifting case. Or his underage speeding citation on Highway 210 that put me in front of a judge at the Crow Wing County Courthouse in February 2023, right when I was already on that PIP from Karen Olson and the truck was acting up.

Stick to operations, safety, and budget in the meeting. Keep the rest in here.

To: jenna.thompson@ci.brainerd.mn.us
Subject: Truck replacement item – getting it on the 3/16 agenda

Hi Jenna,

I’m sliding the agenda request for the Unit #27 replacement over to you a little closer to the deadline than I’d like. To be honest, I haven’t had time to dig into every piece of supporting documentation yet – between the January 11 hydraulic blowout on Laurel Street by Essentia Health–Brainerd Clinic and a couple of snow events stacked back‑to‑back, I’ve been in triage mode. My thought is to at least get it on the March 16, 2026 Council agenda and keep refining the backup materials over the next week.

The timing is important for a few reasons. When that hydraulic line blew on 1/11, I aggravated the lower back injury I’ve been treating at Cuyuna Regional Medical Center’s Occupational Health Clinic since my work comp claim opened in May 2024. Dr. Karen Ellingson has me on a limit of climbing onto the box no more than 2–3 times per shift. Every time #27 fails, I’m either up there myself or pulling another operator off route, and neither option is sustainable.

On top of that, my therapist, Dr. Melissa Hart at Lakes Area Behavioral Health, told me after the February 2 near‑miss by Brainerd High School – when #27 slid toward a pedestrian and I had a full panic episode – that continuing to supervise a truck I don’t trust is feeding my anxiety and sleep issues.

Appreciate you squeezing this into the packet. Let me know if there’s anything you need from me before it goes to Kelly.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Truck replacement item – getting it on the 3/16 agenda

artifacts.content:
To: dan.meyer@cityofduluth.gov
Subject: Politics, plow trucks, and neighborhood blowback

Hey Dan,

Thanks again for sending over your spec sheet on the HV507s you guys bought last year. It helped us justify the Boyer Trucks quote for our own replacement.

We’re finally moving the Unit #27 replacement forward, but it’s messy. The loudest voices are coming from one particularly vocal neighborhood group around Oak and Pine. They tend to dominate public comment and social media, even though our route data don’t show they’re getting worse service than the rest of the city. We’ve talked, internally, about just riding it out and hoping their complaints taper off, but that would be more about dodging political heat than fixing the underlying reliability issues.

I’m also bracing for the fact that this proposal will probably tick off some folks on Oak and Pine who already feel like the City ignores them. Right now I’m not planning any outreach beyond the required notices unless Council specifically asks for more – staff bandwidth is shot between retirements and the 2026 construction prep.

On the home front, things are…busy. Noah’s juggling hockey, his part‑time gig at the hardware store, and that underage DUI mess from February 9 on Washington Street. He’ll start a court‑ordered outpatient program at Northern Pines in early July with twice‑weekly evening sessions, so I’m trying to get big equipment decisions done before I’m playing chauffeur to treatment on top of everything else.

Anyway, if you have any tips on managing the politics around plow routes and “forgotten” neighborhoods, I’m all ears.

Take care,
Mike

metadata.subject: Politics, plow trucks, and neighborhood blowback

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Larson <mlarson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Emily Larson <emily.larson@isd181.org>
Date: April 6, 2023
Subject: Doctor, storms, and this plow truck mess

Em,

Yesterday’s appointment with Dr. Lisa Hendrickson at Brainerd Medical Center was a wake‑up call. She flat‑out said the 18–20 hour stretches I pulled at the City Garage on Wright Street during the March 2022 and January 2023 storms – babysitting Unit #27 every time it broke down – likely pushed my blood pressure into the hypertension range and aggravated the sleep apnea. She put it in the April 5, 2023 chart note and told me to avoid those marathon overnights whenever possible.

That’s part of why I’m pushing so hard on this International HV507 SFA purchase from Boyer Trucks in Duluth. The motion I’m drafting will authorize the plow truck purchase and disposal of Unit #27 for an amount not to exceed $313,000 from the 2026 Public Works Capital Equipment budget. If we can get that through Council, I won’t keep losing standby pay like I did after the January 2024 storm when the old truck was down and I couldn’t claim the hours. Remember how we ended up pulling $2,400 from the kids’ 529 at Affinity Plus Federal Credit Union to cover the transmission repair on the 2012 F‑150?

We opened that 529 back in March 2011 for Noah and Claire’s college, not to patch holes from broken equipment and overtime cuts. I’m tired of raiding it and tired of feeling like my health is collateral damage every time it snows.

Talk tonight?

– Mike

metadata.subject: Doctor, storms, and this plow truck mess

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Larson <mlarson@ci.brainerd.mn.us>
To: Sarah Peterson <sarah.peterson@gmail.com>
Date: November 12, 2025
Subject: Dad, work, and the plow truck situation

Sarah,

I’ve been meaning to write this out instead of just texting bits and pieces. Dad’s stroke on October 19, 2025 at St. Joseph’s Medical Center really drove home how thin we are running things here. I spent three weeks in and out of his rehab at Good Samaritan Society–Bethany in Brainerd, trying to be there for him and Mom, and I was still fielding angry calls on my cell about missed alley plowing because Unit #27 kept acting up and we had no reliable backup.

It brought back January 15, 2024, when I had to walk away from the night plow shift to get Dad from his South 7th Street duplex to the Essentia Health–St. Joseph’s Medical Center ER after he slipped on unplowed ice and fractured his hip. Medicare only covered part of the ER and rehab, and Good Samaritan Society–Bethany’s billing office says they’re still on the hook for over $8,000 out of pocket from that hip fracture stay.

I’m drafting a motion for Council to adopt the resolution authorizing purchase of the International HV507 SFA and disposal of Unit #27. Officially it’s about reliability and budgets, but personally it’s about not juggling crisis caregiving and equipment failures again. If I have to take extended family leave next time, I want the Streets Division to have enough dependable trucks that alleys still get cleared without me answering complaints from Dad’s hospital room.

Love,

Mike

metadata.subject: Dad, work, and the plow truck situation

artifacts.content:
Personal planning memo – Unit #27 replacement talking points
Author: Michael Mark Larson
Date: February 3, 2026

Do NOT put all of this in the public staff report, but I need it in one place to remind myself what’s really at stake.

1) Claire / December 9–10, 2023 blizzard
During the Dec 9–10, 2023 storm, I missed half a plow shift so I could be with Emily at Claire’s emergency counseling intake at Northern Pines Mental Health Center in Brainerd. That was after her panic attack when a car slid toward her on icy Oak Street. The intake counselor was clear both parents needed to be there. With Unit #27 down again, we barely covered routes. Motion language (internal draft): “Motion to adopt the resolution authorizing the purchase of the International HV507 SFA plow truck and disposal of Unit #27 so that, unlike during the December 9–10, 2023 blizzard when I missed half a shift to attend my daughter Claire’s emergency counseling intake at Northern Pines after a near‑miss on Oak Street, we have enough reliable equipment to cover sudden staffing gaps.”

2) Money / 529 plan
Remember: we opened Noah and Claire’s 529 at Affinity Plus Federal Credit Union (Brainerd branch) in March 2011. I’ve already borrowed against it more than once, including the $2,400 transmission repair bill on the 2012 F‑150 after the January 2024 storm when I couldn’t claim standby pay because the old truck was down. Budget note to self: stop raiding the kids’ college savings to backfill income lost to equipment failures.

3) Dad incidents
Keep Dad’s January 15, 2024 hip fracture and the October 19, 2025 stroke in mind as personal motivation, but don’t reference those medical details in the actual Council motion.

metadata.title: Personal planning memo – Unit #27 replacement talking points
